conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions. Let me start by gathering some basic information:

Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child I'm inquiring about. Their name is Alex and they're 19 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Since Alex is 19 years old, I need to ask a few more questions to determine eligibility, as Child Benefit eligibility changes after age 16.

Is Alex currently in approved education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is still in education. They're doing their A-levels at sixth form college.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. That's helpful to know. 

Can you tell me when Alex's course is expected to finish, and are they enrolled in full-time education?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, they're enrolled full-time. The A-levels course should finish in the summer - so in a few months' time when they complete their exams.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Now I need to ask about your own circumstances:

Are you the person responsible for Alex - meaning, does Alex live with you, or do you contribute at least the same amount as Child Benefit would pay towards their upkeep?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives with me at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. I need to understand a bit more about your residency status:

Do you live in the UK, and are you considered a UK resident for tax purposes? (Or do you have the right to claim Child Benefit through working in the UK or receiving certain UK benefits?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester. I'm a UK resident.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing that information. Before I can make a final determination, let me just read through the official guidance to ensure I have all the details correct.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. I have one more important question:

Is Alex continuing their education beyond the summer, or will they be stopping education once they finish their A-levels?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: They'll be finishing their A-levels in the summer and that's the end of their course. They haven't decided yet what they're doing after that - might go to university or get a job.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have enough details to make a determination about your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on the official guidance, let me explain the outcome:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! You are currently eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's what you need to know:

✅ **Current eligibility:** You qualify for Child Benefit for Alex because:
- Alex is 19 years old (under 20)
- Alex is in full-time approved education (A-levels)
- Alex lives with you
- You are a UK resident

⚠️ **Important information about when the A-levels finish:**
- Your Child Benefit will **stop** when Alex finishes their A-levels in the summer, unless Alex continues into further approved education or training
- If Alex goes to university and continues in full-time approved education, your Child Benefit can continue
- If Alex leaves education and doesn't continue, you may be able to claim Child Benefit for up to **20 weeks** if Alex registers with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services

**What to do next:**
- Make sure to inform HMRC when Alex finishes their A-levels in the summer
- Let them know what Alex's plans are (university, work, etc.) so they can advise on continued eligibility

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 19 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 19 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 19 years old and currently enrolled full-time in approved education (A-levels at sixth form college). According to the eligibility rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. Alex lives with you (so you are responsible for them), and you are a UK resident living in the UK. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex while they remain in approved full-time education. However, when Alex finishes their A-levels in the summer, your Child Benefit will stop unless Alex continues into further approved education or training. If Alex leaves education at that point and registers with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services, you can apply for Child Benefit to continue for up to 20 weeks.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 19-year-old child Alex, who is currently in full-time approved education (A-levels). The claimant lives in the UK, is a UK resident, and Alex lives with them. Eligibility will continue until Alex completes their A-levels in the summer, after which Child Benefit will stop unless Alex enters further approved education/training or registers with certain services (which would extend eligibility for up to 20 weeks).

According to the eligibility rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. Alex lives with you (so you are responsible for them), and you are a UK resident living in the UK. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex while they remain in approved full-time education. However, when Alex finishes their A-levels in the summer, your Child Benefit will stop unless Alex continues into further approved education or training. If Alex leaves education at that point and registers with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services, you can apply for Child Benefit to continue for up to 20 weeks.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 19-year-old child Alex, who is currently in full-time approved education (A-levels). The claimant lives in the UK, is a UK resident, and Alex lives with them. Eligibility will continue until Alex completes their A-levels in the summer, after which Child Benefit will stop unless Alex enters further approved education/training or registers with certain services (which would extend eligibility for up to 20 weeks).
